content.full_text:
Re
Fnrn PolrrrcAr- PnecrrcBs CoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 . Sacramento. CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
December 30, 2010
Marjorie Baxter
Senior Assistant City Attomey
2979 Tapo Canyon Road
Simi Valley, CA 93063-2199
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our file No. I-10-195
Dear Ms. Baxter:
QUESTTON
Does a city employee who presents information regarding grant applicants to the
Community Development Block Grant Advisory Committee, which makes recommendations to
the Simi Valley City Council (the "City Council"), have a disqualifying conflict of interest based
on her property's proximity to the property of a potential grant recipient?
CONCLUSION
' 
No. The city employee does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest; based on the
facts provided, the city employee is not making, participating in, or influencing a cognizable
governmental decision.
' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 1000 through 9 I 0 14. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
r Infbrmal assistance cloes not offer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or tormal written
advice. (Regulation I 8329(c)(3).)
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We base this letter on the facts presented;
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when
it renders advice. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice
solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-
of-interest laws. Because you are seeking advice third party advice, we can provide you only
with informal assistance.'


File No. I-10-195
Page No. 2
FACTS
Through written advice and during a telephone call, you provided the following facts. As
the City Attorney for the City of Simi Valley, you represent the Deputy Director of Housing,
Rob Bnrce, who has questions about his employee Carolyn Duncan. Ms. Duncan's position is
one of the Community Development Block Grant Coordinators; she is a designated employee.
She processes applications for grant requests that are reviewed by the Community Development
Block Grant Advisory Committee (the "Committee") for eventual decision by the City Council.
The community Development Block Grants is a federally funded program.
As the City's coordinator, Ms. Duncan reviews grant applications for completeness and
prepares a standardized project sllmmary. The summary includes information from the grant
application, including: project description and budget, how the project meets identified National
Objectives established by HUD, and how the project meets the City Council's Consolidated
Plan. The project summary is then reviewed at several levels, presented to the Committee and
then to the City Council. The City Council then reviews the provided information and the
recommendations that the Committee made, and, if necessary, asks the city staff, including
Ms. Duncan, for more information. Ms. Duncan's supervisor, Mr. Bmce is available for consult
by the City Council and intervenes where necessary as his staff provides information.
City staff is not involved with the decision-making on any of the applications. Rather,
city staff, including the coordinators, synthesizes information and present it to the Committee
and the City Council.
Ms. Duncan owns a home in Simi Valley that is within 500 feet of a girls' group home
that applies for grants through the city's block grants program.
Ai\ALYSN
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a goverTlmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.
Step One: Is the individual a "public official?"
The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103: Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "public official" is o'every member, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .." (Section 82048.) As a Communiiy
Development Block Grant Coordinator for Simi Valley, Ms. Duncan is a "public official" under
the Act.


File No. I-10-195
Page No. 3
Step Two: Is the official making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decision-maker
regarding the govemmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, th"-
official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) Regulation 18702.4,however, provides in pertinent part that:
"[m]aking or participating in making a govemmental decision shall not inciude... [a]ctions of
public officials which are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical."
Ms. Duncan's role in the block grant program is to analyze and synthesize information to
facilitate the Committee and the City Council's decisions. She does not act on behalf of her
agency regarding a particular course of action. Her supervisor, Mr. Bruce, intervenes on her
behalf if necessary before the Committee or City Council. Additionally, Ms. Duncan is not
making recommendations, but presenting information so that the Committee, an advisory board,
can make recommendations to the City Council.
For these reasons, we find that the employee, Carolyn Duncan, is not making,
participating in making, or influencing a govemmental decision and that her actions are solely
ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical. Because there is no govemmental decision in which
she is participatin$, we need not analyze the remaining steps to determine that Ms. Duncan does
not have a disqualifying conflict of interest.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
%
M. Rowan
HMR:jgl
el, Legal Division
